chapterSummary.title: A Watering Rhyme / The Giving Tree
chapterSummary.content: Unit 7 of the Marigold textbook brings together two beautiful pieces about our relationship with nature. The poem 'A Watering Rhyme' teaches children the right time and way to water plants - early morning or evening, never during the hot afternoon sun. The story 'The Giving Tree' tells us about a tree that loved a little boy so much that it gave him everything it had - its apples, branches, and trunk. In the end, only a stump was left, but the tree was still happy to offer a seat to the tired old man. The story is a powerful lesson about selfless love and gratitude. The grammar section introduces tenses - past, present, and future - helping students understand how verbs change to show when an action happens.
keyPoints: The poem teaches us to water plants in the morning or evening, not during the hot afternoon
Plants need regular care with water, sunlight, and love to grow well
The Giving Tree gave the boy apples, branches, and its trunk as he grew older
The story teaches selfless love - giving without expecting anything in return
Past tense tells about actions that already happened (played, climbed, gave)
Present tense tells about actions happening now (plays, climbs, gives)
Future tense uses 'will' or 'shall' for actions that will happen later
learningObjectives: Understand the poem A Watering Rhyme and explain the best times to water plants
Retell the story of The Giving Tree and explain its moral lesson
Identify themes of selfless love and gratitude in the story
Recognise past, present, and future tense in sentences
Change verbs from one tense to another correctly
Know the difference between regular and irregular past tense forms

curatedNotes.title: Plant Care Journal Activity
curatedNotes.content: Start a small plant care journal! Pick a plant at home or in school. Water it at the right time (morning or evening) every day for a week. Write down what you did each day using past tense: 'I watered the plant in the morning. The leaves looked fresh.' This helps you practise both caring for nature and using tenses!

curatedNotes.title: The Giving Tree and Your Life
curatedNotes.content: Think about the people in your life who are like the Giving Tree - your parents, grandparents, or teachers. They give you love, food, education, and care without asking for anything in return. Write a short thank-you note to someone who is like a giving tree in your life. Use at least one sentence each in past, present, and future tense.

curatedNotes.title: Tense Spotting Challenge
curatedNotes.content: Read 'The Giving Tree' story again and make three columns: Past, Present, Future. Write every verb you find in the correct column. For example: 'climbed' goes in Past, 'gives' goes in Present. Can you find verbs for all three tenses? This is a great way to practise identifying tenses in real stories!
